us-politics.headline: US Warplanes Shot Down Over Iran, Crew Member Missing
us-politics.body: Two US fighter jets and two helicopters were hit by Iranian fire, with search operations ongoing for at least one missing crew member. The incidents mark a serious escalation in US military operations against Iran and have drawn sharp scrutiny from Congress. Republican Rep. Nancy Mace voiced concern about escalation, while GOP Sen. John Curtis called for ending offensive operations without a formal declaration of war. NPR notes Trump's wartime rhetoric diverges from past presidents in tone and legal framing, a contrast supporters say reflects resolve and critics call reckless. A Democratic congressman separately condemned prediction market Polymarket for taking bets on the missing pilot's fate.

sources.title: What to know about US fighter jets hit in Iran war
2 US helicopters hit during recovery efforts in Iran
GOP senator calls to end Iran operations without formal declaration of war
Two U.S. warplanes shot down; search ongoing in Iran for 1 missing crew member
Mace ‘concerned about escalation’ in Iran war
Democrat rips Polymarket for taking bets on fate of US pilot shot down over Iran
How Trump's wartime rhetoric differs from past presidents and why that matters

us-politics.headline: Trump Budget, Cabinet Shifts and DOJ Leadership Draw Fire
us-politics.body: Trump's proposed federal budget includes a $10 billion fund for Washington DC construction and a significant defense spending outline, but Republican Sen. Susan Collins broke with the White House over funding cuts she says go too far. Trump is also weighing Cabinet changes while trying to avoid a broad shake-up, with figures including Howard Lutnick and Todd Blanche in focus. Blanche, now heading the Justice Department, told reporters there is no evidence Jeffrey Epstein was a foreign spy, though Rep. Mace is still pushing for former AG Pam Bondi to testify on the Epstein files. Sen. Tillis separately set a Jan. 6 red line for any incoming attorney general nominee.

sources.title: Trump weighs more Cabinet changes, but wants to avoid ‘massive shake-up’
Todd Blanche takes on DOJ’s top job and the burden of Trump’s expectations
Susan Collins knocks funding cuts in White House budget proposal
What to know about Trump’s defense budget proposal
Blanche: No evidence in Epstein files he was a spy
Senate Democrats press Treasury Department on decision to add Trump name to currency
Tillis sets Jan. 6 red line on attorney general nominee
Trump proposes $10B fund for D.C. construction, beautification projects
Mace ‘not backing away’ from Bondi testifying on Epstein

us-politics.headline: States Sue Over Mail-In Voting Order; Colorado Lawyers Protest ICE Pledge
us-politics.body: Several Democratic-led states filed suit against Trump's executive order restricting vote-by-mail, with Oregon's attorney general calling it a source of "confusion, chaos and distrust." The legal challenges argue the order oversteps federal authority over state election administration. Separately, lawyers in Colorado report that the state court's electronic filing system now requires them to certify they will not assist ICE - a requirement critics say puts legal professionals in an impossible position. Supporters of the ICE provision argue it protects enforcement operations; opponents say it compromises attorney independence.

sources.title: ‘Confusion, chaos and distrust’: Oregon challenges Trump’s order restricting vote-by-mail
Colorado lawyers say court e-file system now makes them certify they won’t assist ICE
Democratic-led states sue over Trump mail-in ballot order

us-politics.headline: Trump Signs College Sports Order; GOP Praises Ousted Army Chief
us-politics.body: Trump signed an executive order targeting college sports governance, framing it as a fix to what he called a broken system following the NIL era and conference realignment. The order drew mixed reactions, with supporters welcoming federal intervention and critics questioning whether the White House has clear authority over NCAA rules. On a separate front, Republican lawmakers offered rare public praise for Army Chief of Staff Gen. Randy George after Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth removed him - an unusual moment of GOP pushback against a Pentagon personnel decision. Trump also issued a Good Friday message calling for a "resurgence of religion," drawing praise from conservative commentators.

sources.title: Trump signs order aimed at reforming college sports
Trump says ‘America needs God’ in Good Friday message touting ‘resurgence of religion'
Trump again orders changes to ‘save’ college sports
Trump gives rare praise to persistent GOP foe after White House ballroom vote
GOP praise pours in for Army chief of staff ousted by Hegseth

au-politics.headline: Fuel fears ease but panic buying keeps demand high
au-politics.body:
Energy Minister Chris Bowen says fewer service stations are running dry, pointing to strong national fuel reserves. But demand remains elevated as motorists continue panic buying, undermining the supply picture on the ground.

The Spectator argues the government's response has been overblown, suggesting drivers simply check tyre pressure rather than worry about shortages. Farmers have used the moment to push supermarkets for better prices on fresh produce, linking fuel cost pressures to the broader squeeze on agricultural supply chains.

The divide is clear: the government frames the situation as stable and improving, critics on the right see unnecessary alarm, and primary producers want structural change regardless of how the fuel story resolves.

sources.title: Fewer service stations running out of petrol and diesel as Australia’s fuel supplies remain strong, energy minister says
Just, you know, check your tyre pressure
Fuel demand stays high, as farmers urge supermarkets to pay more for fresh produce

au-politics.headline: Nine charged over major cocaine and meth smuggling operation
au-politics.body:
Nine people face charges after police uncovered an alleged conspiracy to import large quantities of cocaine and methamphetamine into Australia using a so-called mother ship operating in Australian waters. A sunken trawler off Victoria's coast triggered the investigation.

Authorities say the syndicate planned to transfer drugs at sea before bringing them ashore. The scale of the alleged operation - involving tonnes of narcotics - marks it as one of the more significant maritime drug interceptions in recent years.

No political controversy surrounds the arrests, but the case will likely renew debate about Australia's maritime border surveillance capabilities and the resourcing of federal law enforcement.

sources.title: Nine charged over alleged conspiracy to import tonnes of cocaine and meth via ‘mother ship’ in Australian waters
Sunken trawler alerts police to alleged drug-smuggling plan

au-politics.headline: Iran shoots down US aircraft, raising stakes in Middle East
au-politics.body:
Iran has downed two US aircraft, a move analysts say will shock American public opinion and test the Trump administration's response calculus. The incident carries echoes of past moments where US military losses abroad triggered sharp domestic political pressure.

ABC analysis identifies three ways the event could reverberate inside the United States - through public sentiment, congressional pressure, and the administration's credibility on foreign policy. Trump faces a difficult choice between escalation and restraint.

The shootdown comes amid already high tensions and adds a volatile new element to a region where US positioning has been contested. How Washington responds in the coming days will define the immediate trajectory.

sources.title: Iran's downing of two US aircraft will shock Americans in three ways

au-politics.headline: Queensland aged care CEO pay draws scrutiny amid sector pressures
au-politics.body:
Queensland residential aged care executives are earning up to $679,000 a year, according to new figures, raising questions about how sector funding is being distributed. The data lands as the aged care industry continues to argue it is underfunded following the royal commission.

Critics will point to the pay gap between executives and frontline care workers, many of whom remain on relatively modest wages despite recent award increases. Providers argue competitive executive salaries are necessary to attract capable leadership in a complex, regulated environment.

The figures are likely to feature in ongoing political debate about aged care funding adequacy and accountability, particularly given the federal government's post-royal commission reform commitments.

sources.title: Qld residential care sector CEOs paid up to $679k

world.headline: Iran-Israel War Escalates With Missiles, Downed Warplanes
world.body:
Iran launched missile strikes on residential areas in central Israel while Tehran claimed to have shot down two US warplanes, marking a sharp escalation in the conflict. Israel responded by bombing bridges in Lebanon, and the WHO reported more than 20 attacks on Iranian healthcare facilities since March 1. A rescue team is conducting what officials describe as a harrowing search for a missing US crew member inside Iran.

The Strait of Hormuz remains a flashpoint. A French-owned ship passed through the waterway as questions mount over whether force will be used to reopen it. An attack on a UAE gas facility at Habshan killed at least one person, underscoring how the war is radiating outward across the region.

sources.title: Iranian missile attack hits residential areas in central Israel
Rescue team in Iran face 'harrowing and dangerous' search for US crew member
Iran war live: Tehran downs 2 US warplanes; Israel bombs Lebanon bridges
Will force be used to reopen Strait of Hormuz?
Survivors of Iran bridge attack recall terror of US-Israeli strike
More than 20 attacks on Iranian healthcare facilities since March 1: WHO
At least one killed at UAE’s Habshan gas facility after intercepted attack
French-owned ship passes through Strait of Hormuz

world.headline: Gaza and Lebanon Christians Mark Good Friday Amid Bombardment
world.body:
Palestinian Christians in Gaza observed Good Friday under Israeli military operations, while Lebanese Christians held services as Israeli strikes continued nearby. In Jerusalem, monks conducted Easter prayers in an almost empty Old City - a stark image of how the war has hollowed out religious life across the region.

Survivors of an Iranian bridge strike described the terror of what they called a US-Israeli attack. The overlapping religious observances and ongoing violence drew sharp attention to civilian life caught inside an expanding conflict now touching Gaza, Lebanon, Iran, and the UAE.

sources.title: Palestinian Christians in Gaza mark Good Friday
Christians in Lebanon observe Good Friday under Israeli attacks
Monks hold Easter prayers in deserted Old City of Jerusalem
Survivors of Iran bridge attack recall terror of US-Israeli strike

world.headline: Cuba Frees 2,000 Prisoners as US Pressure Bites
world.body: Cuba began releasing more than 2,000 prisoners as the United States intensified diplomatic and economic pressure on Havana. The releases signal a notable concession from a government that has long resisted external demands, though analysts remain cautious about whether the move reflects a deeper political shift or a tactical response to mounting strain.

sources.title: Cuba begins releasing more than 2,000 prisoners as US pressure mounts
Cuba releases over 2,000 prisoners amid mounting US pressure

world.headline: US Political Battles: Ballot Lawsuits and Fed Chair Subpoenas
world.body: More than two dozen Democrat-led states filed suit against the Trump administration over restrictions on mail-in ballots, the latest in a string of legal challenges to federal election policy. Separately, a US judge upheld a decision to throw out subpoenas targeting Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, blocking what critics saw as an attempt to pressure the central bank. US unemployment fell despite economic uncertainty and the ongoing Iran war, offering the administration a rare piece of positive economic news.

sources.title: Two dozen Democrat-led states sue Trump over mail-in ballot limits
US judge upholds decision to toss subpoenas into Fed Chair Jerome Powell
US unemployment rate drops despite economic uncertainty and Iran war

ai.headline: Anthropic's Big Week: Biotech Deal, PAC, and Claude Controversy
ai.body:
Anthropic is moving fast on multiple fronts. The company reportedly acquired biotech startup Coefficient Bio for $400 million, launched a political action committee to expand its Washington presence, and is attracting intense private market interest - though SpaceX's anticipated IPO may soon compete for the same investor dollars.

Not everything went smoothly. Anthropic effectively blocked Claude Code subscribers from using OpenClaw, a third-party client, by requiring an additional payment tier. The move drew backlash, and the controversy deepened when security researchers flagged that OpenClaw users should assume their credentials may be compromised following a separate vulnerability disclosure. A busy week for the AI lab - and not entirely a good one.

sources.title: Anthropic is having a moment in the private markets; SpaceX could spoil the party
Anthropic essentially bans OpenClaw from Claude by making subscribers pay extra
Tell HN: Anthropic no longer allowing Claude Code subscriptions to use OpenClaw
OpenClaw gives users yet another reason to be freaked out about security
Anthropic buys biotech startup Coefficient Bio in $400M deal: Reports
Anthropic ramps up its political activities with a new PAC

ai.headline: OpenAI Reshuffles Leadership as Key Executives Step Back
ai.body:
OpenAI is reorganizing at the top. COO Brad Lightcap is moving into a new 'special projects' role, while Fidji Simo, who joined less than a year ago to lead the company's consumer products push, is taking medical leave. The head of OpenAI's AGI division is also stepping back.

The simultaneous departures raise questions about internal stability at a company already navigating a high-stakes transition from nonprofit to for-profit structure. OpenAI has not detailed timelines for any of the absences or named interim replacements.

sources.title: OpenAI executive shuffle includes new role for COO Brad Lightcap to lead ‘special projects’
OpenAI’s AGI boss is taking a leave of absence
OpenAI’s Fidji Simo Is Taking Medical Leave Amid an Executive Shake-Up

ai.headline: AI's Energy Hunger Collides With Politics and Public Pushback
ai.body:
The infrastructure behind AI is running into serious headwinds. Trump's own tariffs are undermining his administration's data center buildout push, raising costs on the imported equipment needed to build them. Meanwhile, AI companies are turning to large natural gas plants to meet power demand - a bet that carries both climate and regulatory risk.

Public sentiment isn't helping. A new survey finds people would sooner accept an Amazon warehouse near their home than a data center. Community opposition, grid constraints, and policy incoherence are converging into a genuine bottleneck for an industry that has treated infrastructure as an afterthought.

sources.title: Trump ignores biggest reasons his AI data center buildout is failing
AI companies are building huge natural gas plants to power data centers. What could go wrong?
People would rather have an Amazon warehouse in their backyard than a data center

ai.headline: Research Warns AI Is Eroding Users' Independent Thinking
ai.body:
A new study finds that heavy AI users are increasingly willing to outsource their reasoning entirely to large language models, a pattern researchers call 'cognitive surrender.' Participants shown AI-generated answers tended to accept them without scrutiny, even when the answers were wrong.

The finding lands at an awkward moment for the industry. AI companies are racing to embed their tools deeper into daily workflows, from coding to medical advice, while evidence mounts that reliance may come at a cost to critical thinking. The research does not argue against AI use, but it does suggest that how people are trained to interact with these systems matters enormously.

sources.title: "Cognitive surrender" leads AI users to abandon logical thinking, research finds

btc.headline: Circle Faces Heat Over $285M Hack and Compliance Failures
btc.body:
Circle is under serious pressure after the $285 million Drift protocol hack, with critics attacking the stablecoin issuer for refusing to freeze the stolen USDC. On-chain investigator ZachXBT went further, accusing Circle of $420 million in compliance failures stretching back to 2022, suggesting the Drift incident is part of a broader pattern.

The backlash puts Circle in an uncomfortable position ahead of its anticipated IPO. The core tension is structural - USDC's freeze capability exists precisely for situations like this, and Circle's inaction raises hard questions about when, and for whom, that power actually gets used.

sources.title: Circle under fire after $285 million Drift hack over inaction to freeze stolen USDC
ZachXBT accuses Circle of $420M in 'compliance failures' since 2022

btc.headline: Schwab Enters Spot Crypto Trading as Institutional Adoption Deepens
btc.body:
Charles Schwab plans to launch direct spot trading for Bitcoin and Ether in the first half of 2026, a move that brings one of America's largest retail brokerages into a market it long kept at arm's length. The timing matters - Schwab's entry signals that spot crypto is becoming a standard offering rather than a niche product.

The announcement lands alongside the Ethereum Foundation closing in on its 70,000 staked ETH target, reflecting growing institutional confidence in Ethereum's infrastructure. Together, the two developments suggest the on-ramp for mainstream capital into crypto is widening fast.

sources.title: Schwab plans spot bitcoin, ether trading launch in first half of 2026
Ethereum Foundation nearly reaches 70,000 staked ETH goal

btc.headline: Quantum Threat, Iran Risk - Bitcoin's Macro Blind Spots Examined
btc.body:
A study claiming quantum computers could crack Bitcoin in nine minutes made headlines, but analysts urge caution - the research describes a theoretical attack on isolated keys under ideal conditions, not a practical threat to the live network. The gap between laboratory benchmarks and real-world cryptographic risk remains vast.

Separately, a former hedge fund manager argues Bitcoin is mispricing the risk of a prolonged Iran conflict, suggesting markets are treating geopolitical escalation as a short-term noise event rather than a structural threat. Both stories point to the same underlying question: which risks is Bitcoin actually pricing in?

sources.title: Here's what 'cracking' bitcoin in 9 minutes by quantum computers actually means
Is Bitcoin mispricing a prolonged Iran war? Ex-hedge fund manager weighs in

btc.headline: Coinbase Trust Charter Draws Banking Opposition as Crypto Regulation Heats Up
btc.body:
US community banks are pushing back against the OCC's approval of a trust charter for Coinbase, arguing it gives the crypto exchange a regulatory foothold that bypasses traditional banking oversight. The opposition reflects a broader turf war as crypto firms pursue bank-adjacent licenses.

Meanwhile, Cambodia is moving to impose severe prison sentences on crypto scammers, and prediction market platform Kalshi has hired a former Democratic strategist as it navigates a tangle of state and federal legal challenges. Across jurisdictions, regulators and lawmakers are tightening their grip on the industry from multiple directions at once.

sources.title: US community banks oppose OCC's approval of Coinbase trust charter
Cambodian lawmakers propose severe prison time for crypto scammers
Kalshi onboards ex-Democratic strategist amid legal troubles

food.name: Miso-Glazed Black Cod
food.description: Silky, buttery cod fillets marinated in white miso, mirin, and sake then broiled until caramelised and lacquered
food.tip: Marinate for at least 24 hours — the longer the fish sits, the deeper the umami penetration and the better the glaze caramelises under the broiler

exercise.name: The Saturday Deck of Cards
exercise.equipment: A standard deck of playing cards, some floor space
exercise.description: Assign a bodyweight move to each suit — push-ups, squats, lunges, burpees — then flip cards one by one and perform the reps shown, turning a lazy Saturday into a genuinely unpredictable full-body circuit

learn.topic: The Ship of Theseus and Personal Identity
learn.description: The ancient paradox asks whether an object that has had all its components replaced remains fundamentally the same object — and when applied to humans, whose cells and memories constantly change, it becomes a sharp question about what 'you' actually are
learn.why: On a quiet Saturday it's the perfect rabbit hole: it connects Greek philosophy, Derek Parfit's modern work on personal identity, and even debates in neuroscience about the continuity of consciousness — one hour in and you'll question what you mean every time you say 'I'
